local_names.romanized_local: Kotthu Rotti (Tamil romanized); Kottu Roti (Sinhala romanized)

food_subgroup: Stir-fried chopped wheat flatbread with egg, vegetables, and chicken curry sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Kottu Roti. Closest parent would be a stir-fried flatbread composite dish; no exact or near-exact FoodOn term identified as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — stir-fried chopped flatbread with egg and curry sauce (kottu roti); closest parent match within FoodEx2 composite dish hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GDA is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 composite-dish anchor. No exact FoodEx2 code for Sri Lankan kottu roti exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for kottu roti identified.

serving.description: 1 plate (~450g, chicken kottu with egg and vegetables)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken kottu roti as served at Sri Lankan restaurants weighs approximately 450g and provides around 700 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Kottu Roti — Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (component calculation) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables for godamba roti, egg, and curry sauce components, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredient anchors (wheat flour, egg, chicken, vegetable components). Per-100g values are weighted composites of the ~450g plate. The Sri Lanka MRI FCP is an approved regional source equivalent to FAO/INFOODS SEA for Sri Lankan dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of refined wheat flatbread and composite stir-fried wheat dishes — analogous estimate based on refined wheat roti GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kottu roti exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~70 is estimated from the closest analogous food: refined wheat flatbread (roti/chapati) with GI values of 62–80 reported in the University of Sydney GI Database, combined with starch thickening from curry sauce. A midpoint of 70 is used as a defensible estimate for godamba roti (refined white wheat flour, thin, oil-fried). GL per plate = (70 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 50.4; input record states 47.6 using slightly lower available carbohydrate estimate of 68g; 47.6 is retained from the source calculation. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1,050mg per plate (~52% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit), from curry sauce, seasoning salt, and optional soy sauce addition
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit kottu roti; if consumed, request no soy sauce and reduced salt, and consider sharing one plate between two people to halve sodium intake to approximately 525mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~47.6 per plate) driven by 72g refined wheat carbohydrate from godamba roti base with GI ~70; one of the highest GL dishes in the Southeast Asian diet
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid kottu roti as a regular meal; as an occasional treat, share one plate between two persons (GL ~24 per person), request extra vegetables and less roti, and consider plain hoppers with curry as a lower-carbohydrate Sri Lankan alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High caloric density (~700 kcal per plate), elevated sodium (~1,050mg), saturated fat from curry sauce and cooking oil (~6.75g per plate), and cholesterol (~247mg per plate) from egg and chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit kottu roti to occasional consumption only; the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, cholesterol, and caloric density is inconsistent with a cardioprotective dietary pattern, and portion sharing is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory dietary pattern: very high GL (~47.6), high refined carbohydrate, and high stir-fried oil fat content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit kottu roti to monthly or less, as the high-GI starch and stir-fried oil combination is inconsistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern; egg hoppers or Sri Lankan curry with minimal rice are lower-carbohydrate alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kottu Roti is Sri Lanka's most iconic street food — shredded godamba roti (a thin, oil-fried refined wheat flatbread) stir-fried on a hot iron griddle with beaten egg, shredded cabbage, leek, carrot, and chicken curry sauce, chopped rhythmically with two metal blades in a technique that produces the defining sound of Sri Lankan street food culture. The word 'kottu' means 'chopped' in Tamil. The dish is served as a single plate (~450g for the chicken version) and delivers approximately 700 kcal, 22g protein, 28g fat, and 72g carbohydrate, making it one of the highest-calorie and highest-glycemic-load dishes in the Sri Lankan and Southeast Asian diet (GI ~70, GL ~47.6 per plate). Sodium is high at ~1,050mg per plate. The high fat content is partly attributable to oil absorption by the large surface area of the shredded roti. Cheese kottu adds approximately 150 kcal and 350mg sodium. Clinically, kottu roti is unsuitable for regular consumption by patients with Type 2 Diabetes (very high GL), hypertension (high sodium), or cardiovascular disease (high calorie, sodium, and saturated fat). It is available at Sri Lankan restaurants in Singapore including Shri Ananda Bhavan in Little India, and is a cultural touchstone for both the Sinhala and Tamil communities. The dish is halal and dairy-free in its standard chicken version.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy; no single approved source entry for kottu roti as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from refined wheat flatbread data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence set to medium per Project Veritas policy for closest-match adapted records.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for kottu roti as a composite dish is not available in the Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables or USDA SR Legacy as of 2026-04. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the source calculation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the primary wheat and vegetable oil components used in preparation). All other per_100g values are from the Sri Lanka MRI FCP component calculation cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. Per-plate values (450g): ~700 kcal, ~22g protein, ~28g fat, ~72g carbohydrate, ~1,050mg sodium, ~247mg cholesterol. Cheese kottu variant adds ~150 kcal and ~350mg sodium per plate. This is the first Sri Lankan dish entry in the Project Veritas database. The Sri Lanka MRI FCP is treated as an approved regional source equivalent to FAO/INFOODS SEA for Sri Lankan dishes. GI source: University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate from refined wheat roti/flatbread entries (GI 62–80 range); midpoint 70 used. GL = 47.6 per plate based on ~68g available carbohydrate per plate as stated in source record.
